title: Sharing Warmth
description: A cold winter day. Shimamura mentions she's cold, and Adachi rushes over to warm her up—hands, cheeks, and finally, her heart.
content:
'I hate the cold,' I said, 'but I don't actually hate winter.'

'Mm. I see.'

'When I breathe in winter air, it feels like all the dust in my heart just blows away.'

'Uh-huh… yeah.'

'You're just humoring me, aren't you?'

'N-No! I'm not!'

'Heh. Either way, I still hate being cold. So… warm me up, okay?'

'Wh— I— O-Okay! I'll go right now!'

'Eh?'

'I'm heading over. Just wait for me. Don't freeze.'

'…Sure.'

Adachi actually came. She really came to my house. All because of that one joke she took way too seriously. Honestly… it made her look surprisingly dependable.

'Well… um,' she said, shifting her weight. 'I kept thinking this might be my only chance to see Shimamura during winter break. I didn't want to waste it.'

She must've biked here at full speed—her forehead glistened with faint sweat, and her eyes flickered the way they always did when she was overwhelmed. For Adachi, stable moments were rarer than the unstable ones, so this felt almost normal.

'Did I bother you?' she asked, looking up with this pitiful expression that struck straight at the heart. This girl really was like a puppy—troublesome in the cutest possible way.

'No, you didn't bother me at all. But tell me—how are you planning to warm me up?'

I sat back down on the cushion and motioned for her to come over. If I really wanted warmth, I would've turned on the heater… Yet, somehow, I had switched it off. I don't even know why I did that when I hate the cold.

'Wh-Where are you cold?' Adachi asked, kneeling in front of me with full seriousness.

Was she… actually planning to warm me? That thought alone made choosing an answer harder.

'Hmm… maybe my fingers?'

They weren't even that cold, but whatever. As soon as I held out my hand, Adachi grabbed my fingers—no hesitation, no tentative testing. Just a firm, earnest hold. All her focus pooled into that one point of contact.

She stayed there, hunched forward, cheeks faintly pink—utterly frozen in place like a little figurine.

'This is… a much more direct method than I expected.' I laughed softly, and her ears flushed even redder as she turned her head away.

'A-Are there any… other methods?' she whispered.

There probably were. But if this was Adachi's method, then it was perfect.

'Just like always,' I said.

Our hands bridged the space between us, warming each other as the room grew colder without the heater. The mix of warm and cold air wrapped around my skin, making it tingle. Seal-kun sat half-stuffed under the kotatsu blanket on the other side of the room, looking enviably warm.

'Thanks for sharing your warmth with me. Though your hands are actually colder than mine.' Her fingers, exposed to the winter wind while she biked here, were freezing.

'Eh—ah—um… w-was it warm at all?'

'Well… heat goes both ways.'

If we kept holding hands, both of us would warm up eventually.

We sat there like that—just two girls kneeling across from each other in a dusty second-floor room, wordlessly sharing warmth. Somehow, that alone made me laugh. I didn't know what Adachi was thinking, but the tiny, nervous glances she kept sneaking at my face were hilarious.

'Next… my cheeks are cold,' I said, once her hands felt warm enough.

Adachi perked up like someone had told her she'd done a good job, raised both hands high like she was cheering, then suddenly clamped them onto my cheeks.

Because she'd reached all the way around, the movement was so exaggerated I couldn't help but laugh. My shoulders shook. She bowed her head, unable to meet my eyes—the angle made her look like she was praying to me.

'H-How is it?' Her voice bounced off the floor and brushed against my ears.

'It's… prickly.'

'Prickly?'

'My cheeks feel prickly.'

It felt like brushing my cheek against coarse fabric. Not unpleasant—just a little loud on the skin.

In fact, it was a bit like dunking only my head in warm bathwater. Strange, but oddly soothing.

'I-Is that okay?'

'Probably,' I replied.

I stroked the back of her hand, still pressed against my cheek.

'Then next is… my heart,' I said, testing her.

'My heart is cold.'

My heart wasn't actually cold. I just wanted to see what Adachi would do with something abstract—something you can't touch.

Her arms jerked out and pulled back repeatedly, like she was dancing in panic. She reached toward my face, stopped, shook her head, looked at me, blushed, bowed again.

All right then, my little guardian of warmth—fight on. After a moment, she got to her feet and shuffled closer on her knees.

Then—

Slowly—painfully slow—Adachi opened her arms and folded them around me. It took maybe ten seconds from start to finish. Ten seconds of anticipation, of silence, of winter air. Quiet as falling snow, she wrapped herself around me.

Her body trembled slightly—probably from kneeling too long. Her chest rested gently against my forehead.

How do I even describe this?

'Adachi… you're really warm.'

The words slipped out without permission. Something inside my head clicked into place, like a loose screw tightening.

'T-That's good…' she said weakly, clearly overwhelmed.

The warmth surrounding me was absolutely real. So this is how Adachi imagines my heart, I thought. And strangely, it suited me.

I breathed in her scent. Every time I exhaled against her, she gave a tiny twitch, but I pretended not to notice and drew in more of that faint, wintry warmth that clung to her.

Unlike the dusty cold of winter, the warmth she gave me stayed. It sank into my heart—soft, a little damp—slowly dripping down from my chest, settling deeper and deeper inside me.
